output:
# Density-Conditional Any-Resolution for Grounded Mobile UI Understanding: The ISOPANE Limit of Ferret-UI and the LOUPE Reallocation

**A high-level summarized insight of a research field: Machine Learning (Multimodal LLMs for UI understanding; visual referring and grounding).**

Ferret-UI [1] equips a multimodal LLM with referring and grounding on mobile screens by introducing an "any-resolution" (anyres) front end: because UI screens have elongated aspect ratios and many small objects (icons, text), it splits each screen into sub-images so that a square CLIP encoder can see those small elements at usable magnification. This document analyzes a structural limitation of that front end, names it the **ISOPANE** gap (Isotropic-Pane Allocation limit), and proposes **LOUPE** (Local Observable-density, Uniform-budget Pane rEallocation): a density-conditional, structured, partially-pooled reallocation of the anyres partition that provably collapses to Ferret-UI's fixed grid when on-screen element density is spatially uniform, and that uses no additional visual-token compute. The proposal is then written up in the requested five-question (5q) format.

---

## Section 1. Background and Current State of the Art

### 1.1 Referring and grounding for UI screens

Mobile UI automation requires a model that can both *refer* (use a region of the input - point, box, scribble - to talk about a specific widget) and *ground* (emit precise on-screen locations - bounding boxes - in its outputs) [1]. General-purpose grounding MLLMs such as Ferret [2], Shikra [3], and Kosmos-2 [4] established strong region-level referring/grounding on natural images, typically by representing coordinates as text tokens or via a spatial-aware visual sampler. However, these models are tuned for natural photographs: roughly square aspect ratios and relatively large objects of interest. UI screens violate both assumptions - they are tall and narrow (or wide and short), and the objects of interest (a 24 px icon, a one-line label) are tiny relative to the canvas [1]. Naively resizing a full screen to a 224 px or 336 px encoder input destroys exactly the high-frequency signal (small glyphs, thin separators, dense icon rows) that the downstream tasks depend on.

### 1.2 How Ferret-UI addresses resolution: the anyres front end

Ferret-UI's central architectural move is "any resolution" (anyres) [1]. Given a screen, it (i) inspects the *coarse aspect ratio* to decide an orientation, (ii) splits the screen into two equal sub-images - a top/bottom split for portrait screens (a 1x2 layout) or a left/right split for landscape screens (a 2x1 layout) - and (iii) encodes each half, plus a downsized global thumbnail, with the same CLIP-style encoder [29] (a Vision Transformer backbone [30]) at the same fixed resolution [1, 14, 15]. The per-sub-image visual tokens are concatenated and consumed by the LLM alongside Ferret's spatial-aware visual sampler. This buys roughly a 2x effective magnification for small elements at a controlled token budget, and Ferret-UI couples it with a curated suite of *elementary* tasks (widget classification, icon recognition, OCR; and grounding tasks: find-widget, find-icon, find-text, widget listing) and *advanced* tasks (detailed description, perception/interaction conversation, function inference) [1].

The key property for this analysis: **the anyres partition is a single global rule.** The grid is selected only from coarse orientation; the split is always into equal halves; and every sub-image receives the same encoder resolution (hence the same magnification budget). Nothing about the partition depends on *where the small or dense elements actually are* on the particular screen.

### 1.3 The broader high-resolution MLLM landscape

A fast-moving line of work attacks the resolution bottleneck of MLLMs by slicing high-resolution images into crops. LLaVA-1.5 and the AnyRes recipe popularized fixed multi-crop grids [14, 15]; SPHINX and UReader mix multiple fixed sub-image scales [16, 17]; Monkey uses sliding windows to ingest very large images [18]; LLaVA-UHD performs adaptive image *slicing* that chooses a slice layout from the native aspect ratio and resolution [19]; InternLM-XComposer2-4KHD uses "dynamic resolution" to pick, per image, a global number of patches up to 4K [20]; mPLUG-DocOwl 1.5 introduces a shape-adaptive cropping module for documents [21]. Crucially, these methods adapt the *global* crop layout per image (driven by aspect ratio or total resolution) but still treat the interior **isotropically** - every region of a given image is sliced and magnified by the same rule. None conditions the *intra-image* magnification budget on where the informative small elements are, and none is formulated to reduce exactly to a named base partition.

Orthogonally, the efficient-transformer literature reallocates *tokens* inside an encoder via saliency or redundancy - Token Merging (ToMe) [22], adaptive token sampling, FlexiViT's variable patch size [23] - but these operate on a single global image stream for speed, not on a UI sub-image *partition* whose geometry decides magnification for grounding.

### 1.4 UI agents, grounding, and benchmarks

Downstream UI work either processes whole screens as single inputs (Pix2Struct [5], ILuvUI [6], CogAgent [7], which adds a dedicated high-resolution cross-attention branch), supports a single input box (Spotlight [8]), or drives GPT-4V over screenshots with set-of-mark style prompts (MM-Navigator [9, 24], AppAgent [10], Mobile-Agent [11]). GUI grounding has its own benchmarks: SeeClick (built on Qwen-VL [31]) with the ScreenSpot benchmark spans mobile/desktop/web with explicit icon-vs-text splits [12]; Android-in-the-Wild covers navigation [25]; and classic UI corpora - RICO [13], Screen2Words [26], Widget Captioning [27], and Screen Recognition [28] - provide screens, hierarchies, and element annotations. These benchmarks evaluate primarily with *pooled* accuracy, which - as Section 2 argues - is exactly the measurement that hides the limitation studied here.

### 1.5 A reusable design principle: representational impossibility plus exact collapse

This proposal applies a design principle that recurs across machine-learning subfields: when a method hard-codes a *single global scalar or uniform rule* that is applied identically to heterogeneous units, that scalar is often *representationally incapable* of expressing the per-unit optimum, and the resulting harm is (a) silent under pooled metrics, (b) growing with heterogeneity, and (c) vanishing under homogeneity. The principled fix is to promote the scalar to a *structured, monotone, partially-pooled map* of an *observable* covariate, confined to the original layer, and constructed so that it *provably collapses* back to the base method at a default setting (a do-no-harm guarantee that is unit-testable in CI). Applied to Ferret-UI's anyres front end, the "uniform rule" is the equal-halves, uniform-magnification partition; the observable covariate is on-screen element density; and the collapse target is Ferret-UI itself.

---

## Section 2. Key Findings and Analysis

### 2.1 Anatomy of the ISOPANE gap

Let a screen S be partitioned by Ferret-UI's anyres rule into panes i = 1..n (n = 2) with boundaries at the midpoint, each pane encoded at a fixed resolution R that yields a fixed token budget t per pane, for a total visual-token budget B = sum_i t_i [1]. Define the *effective magnification* of pane i as m_i = (encoder resolution) / (pixels of screen covered by pane i): a smaller crop resized to the same R sees each UI element with more pixels. Ferret-UI fixes:

- the **grid** g in {1x2, 2x1} from coarse orientation only;
- the **boundaries** at the geometric midpoint (equal-area panes);
- the **magnification** m_i = m_global, identical across panes.

Now consider the observable per-location *element-density field* rho(x, y) of the screen - the local concentration of small interactable/text elements (formally, the density of small ground-truth or detected element boxes; at inference, a cheap proxy; Section 3). Real screens are highly *anisotropic* in rho: a status bar and a dense icon toolbar may pack tiny elements into the top 10-15% of a portrait screen while a media canvas or whitespace fills the rest. The *per-region optimal magnification* m*(rho) is monotone increasing in local density and small-element fineness. A single global m_global cannot equal m*(rho) wherever rho is non-uniform: it **over-magnifies** sparse regions (wasting budget on whitespace) and **under-magnifies** dense regions (the very small elements anyres was meant to rescue). This is the ISOPANE gap: an isotropic pane allocation is representationally incapable of expressing a per-region magnification that the screen's own density demands.

### 2.2 Why the harm is SILENT, SCALES, and VANISHES

**SILENT under pooled accuracy.** Standard UI benchmarks report pooled task accuracy averaged over screens and elements [12, 13, 26, 27]. Screens whose elements are spatially spread out are well served by equal halves; the density-skewed tail (toolbars, status bars, dense settings lists, keyboards) is a minority of screens, and within the mean the over-magnification of sparse regions and under-magnification of dense regions partially cancel. The net pooled number barely moves even when small-element grounding on skewed screens is badly degraded. The harm is therefore *invisible to the metric the field reports*.

**SCALES with heterogeneity.** The gap grows with the *density skew* of a screen - quantified, e.g., by the Gini coefficient or coefficient of variation of rho across candidate regions, by aspect-ratio extremity, and by the fraction of sub-threshold-size elements. The more concentrated the small elements, the more a uniform split wastes magnification on emptiness and starves the dense cluster.

**VANISHES under homogeneity.** When rho is spatially uniform, equal-area halves *are* the density-equalizing split and uniform magnification *is* optimal. In that regime the optimal allocation equals Ferret-UI's fixed allocation exactly. This is precisely the condition under which any correct fix must reduce to Ferret-UI - and it motivates the exact-collapse design below.

### 2.3 The C1-C4 wedge

A fix that closes the ISOPANE gap *without* introducing a new failure mode must satisfy four clauses simultaneously:

- **C1 (layer and budget):** operate only at the image-partition / visual-token-allocation layer, with *no* change to the LLM, the referring/grounding heads, the training data format, and - critically - *no increase in the visual-token count or encoder FLOPs* (the allocation is budget-preserving).
- **C2 (observable covariate):** condition the allocation on an observable, cheap-to-compute, pre-LLM covariate - the per-region element-density field rho - not on labels or on an extra heavy model.
- **C3 (structured map):** realize the allocation as a structured, monotone, hierarchically partially-pooled map of rho (one shared functional form, shrunk toward the uniform prior for low-confidence screens), rather than a single global scalar (too rigid) or an unpooled free per-region parameter (too unstable).
- **C4 (exact collapse):** provably reduce, feature-for-feature, to Ferret-UI's fixed equal-halves uniform-magnification partition at a covariate-blind default, gated by E_collapse <= 1e-6 in continuous integration.

**Finding: no prior line covers more than two of C1-C4.** Section 3, Q4 demonstrates this across six near-miss families. Fixed multi-crop anyres [1, 14, 16] satisfies C1 but misses C2/C3/C4; global adaptive-resolution MLLMs [18, 19, 20, 21] move toward C2 at the image level but miss per-region C2, C3, and C4 and typically grow the budget (violating C1); token-merging/foveated encoders [22, 23] miss the UI-density covariate and the collapse; element-cropping UI tools [8, 10, 11] spend extra compute (violating C1) and miss C3/C4. The white space - a budget-preserving, density-conditional, partially-pooled partition map with provable exact collapse to Ferret-UI - is unoccupied.

### 2.4 The exact-collapse identity (feature level)

Let z(S) denote the sequence of visual tokens fed to the LLM. LOUPE defines an allocation A_theta(S) with parameters theta. Construct A so that at theta = 0 (covariate-blind default) the panes are byte-identical to Ferret-UI's equal-halves uniform-resolution panes; then the encoder outputs are identical, so z_LOUPE(S) = z_FerretUI(S) and the model is update-for-update Ferret-UI. The collapse is a *construction*, not an approximation: the conditioning term is exactly zero at the default. The gate

```
E_collapse = max_S || z_LOUPE(S) - z_FerretUI(S) ||_inf  <=  1e-6
```

is asserted by a unit test in CI, guaranteeing zero regression on any screen at the default and on every spatially-uniform screen regardless of theta. This converts "do no harm" from a hope into a build-time invariant.

### 2.5 Glossary

- **rho (element-density field):** observable per-location concentration of small UI/text elements; the covariate.
- **m_i, m_global:** effective magnification of pane i; the uniform value Ferret-UI assigns to all panes.
- **A_theta:** LOUPE's density-conditional allocation (split geometry + per-pane budget).
- **g_theta, h_psi:** monotone non-decreasing maps with g_theta(0) = h_psi(0) = 0 that perturb the split position and per-pane token budget as a function of density imbalance.
- **B:** total visual-token budget; held fixed (budget-preserving).
- **E_collapse:** max feature deviation from Ferret-UI at the default; CI-gated to <= 1e-6.
- **Density skew:** Gini/coefficient-of-variation of rho across candidate regions; the heterogeneity axis along which the gap scales.

---

## Section 3. A Novel Research Idea (5q format)

**[Question 1] - What is the problem?**

Can a per-screen, element-density-conditional, compute-budget-preserving reallocation of Ferret-UI's any-resolution sub-image partition - one that provably collapses to the fixed equal-halves grid when on-screen element density is spatially uniform - improve referring and grounding on small and spatially-concentrated mobile-UI elements without increasing the visual-token budget?

**[Question 2] - Why is it interesting and important?**

*(P1) It reframes a fixed architectural choice as a function of an observable covariate.* Ferret-UI's anyres front end is treated, across the field, as a fixed preprocessing recipe. Recasting the partition as A_theta(rho) - an allocation conditioned on screen element density - turns a hard-coded constant into a learnable, analyzable object. This reframing is the kind that tends to spawn follow-on work: once the community sees the partition as an allocation problem, the same lens applies to crop layout, token budget, and per-region resolution across the entire high-resolution MLLM family [18, 19, 20, 21].

*(P2) It targets the exact regime where UI automation matters most.* The small, densely-packed elements that a uniform split under-magnifies - status-bar icons, toolbars, dense lists, keyboards, settings rows - are disproportionately the *interactable* targets for accessibility tooling, screen readers, automated app testing, and multi-step navigation [1, 11, 25]. Improving small-element grounding is directly improving the actions an agent can reliably take.

*(P3) It makes a hidden failure measurable.* By constructing a *density-skew-stratified* evaluation, the work surfaces a degradation that pooled accuracy hides (Section 2.2). Establishing this stratification as a reporting standard benefits every UI-MLLM paper, much as worst-group and subgroup reporting reshaped robustness and fairness evaluation.

*(P4) It is deployable with zero added inference cost and a do-no-harm guarantee.* Because the allocation is budget-preserving (C1) and collapses exactly to Ferret-UI at the default (C4), it can be shipped as a shadow/opt-in change: it cannot regress uniform screens, and it spends no extra tokens or FLOPs. This is rare for accuracy interventions, which usually trade compute for quality.

*(P5) The principle generalizes.* The promotion of a uniform partition to a monotone, partially-pooled, observable-covariate map with exact collapse is host-agnostic: it transfers to any sliced-image MLLM (LLaVA-NeXT/AnyRes, LLaVA-UHD, XComposer-4KHD, DocOwl) and to non-UI dense-OCR domains (documents, charts, screenshots), making the contribution a template rather than a one-off.

**[Question 3] - Why is it hard?**

*(O1) The per-region optimum is entangled.* The right magnification for a region depends jointly on local density, the physical size of its elements, the content (text vs icon vs image), and the task (OCR vs find-icon vs description). There is no closed-form map from density to magnification; a naive "magnify the densest region" rule can starve a second dense region or over-crop context needed for grounding coordinates.

*(O2) The covariate must be cheap, pre-LLM, and robust (C1, C2).* To preserve the budget and the layer boundary, density must be estimated *before* the encoder, cheaply, and robustly across themes, dark mode, wallpapers, and embedded photos. Raw edge energy is confounded by background imagery; a heavy detector would violate C1. The estimator must be light (a small class-agnostic element/edge detector, an OCR-box prior, or learned saliency distilled from element annotations) yet faithful enough that it points at the small interactable elements, not at decorative texture.

*(O3) The partition is discrete and non-differentiable (C3).* Split position, pane count, and orientation are discrete choices; one cannot backpropagate task loss through a crop operation directly. Closing the gap requires a differentiable relaxation, a search/amortized policy, or a proxy objective (e.g., density-equalization) that provably aligns with the downstream metric.

*(O4) The budget constraint is a hard sum constraint (C1).* Reallocating resolution across panes must hold the total token count and FLOPs fixed. This rules out the easy win of "add more crops"; gains must come from *moving* magnification toward dense regions, realized through split geometry (which is automatically token-neutral) and through per-pane resolution/merge ratios chosen to keep sum_i t_i = B exactly.

*(O5) Lift and exact collapse must hold simultaneously (C4).* The method must improve density-skewed screens while remaining byte-identical to Ferret-UI on uniform screens and at the default. Any technique that perturbs the uniform case (e.g., a learned crop that never reproduces equal halves, or floating-point resampling drift) breaks the collapse guarantee and the do-no-harm property. Engineering exact feature identity at theta = 0 (not merely approximate) is a non-trivial constraint on the relaxation and the resampler.

*(O6) BINDING obstacle - metric blindness.* The deepest reason the problem resists progress is that the standard metric cannot see it. Pooled accuracy averages away the density-skewed tail (Section 2.2), so a method could fully fix dense screens and barely move the headline number, while a method that overfits could inflate it without improving grounding. Without a density-stratified, small-element, compute-matched evaluation - and without the covariate-scramble and uniform-null falsifiers - one cannot even *detect* the gap, let alone attribute a fix to the density signal rather than to extra capacity. Solving the measurement problem is a prerequisite for solving the modeling problem.

**[Question 4] - Why hasn't it been solved before?**

The field's near-misses each fail at least two of C1-C4:

- *(NM1) Fixed multi-crop / anyres (Ferret-UI [1], LLaVA-NeXT/AnyRes [14, 15], SPHINX [16], UReader [17]).* These are the **collapse target**. They satisfy C1 (fixed layer/budget) but choose the grid from aspect ratio alone, with no density conditioning and no per-region map. Misses C2 and C3 (and C4 is trivial - they are the base, not a collapsing generalization).

- *(NM2) Global adaptive-resolution MLLMs (Monkey [18], LLaVA-UHD [19], XComposer2-4KHD [20], DocOwl 1.5 [21]).* These adapt the *global* crop layout or total resolution per image from aspect ratio/size - a step toward C2 at the *image* level - but remain isotropic *within* the image, never allocating magnification to dense *regions*; they generally grow the token budget (violating C1) and have no exact-collapse identity to a named base. Misses per-region C2, C3, and C4 (three clauses).

- *(NM3) Token pruning/merging and foveated/adaptive-token encoders (ToMe [22], FlexiViT [23], adaptive token sampling).* These reallocate or drop tokens *inside* a single global encoder stream by similarity or saliency, primarily to save compute. They do not act on the UI sub-image *partition* geometry, do not use a UI-element-density covariate (generic saliency is not small-interactable-element density), and offer no Ferret-UI grid collapse. Misses C2 and C4, and arguably C1 (encoder-internal, not the partition layer).

- *(NM4) Element-cropping UI specialists and tool-zoom agents (Spotlight single-box input [8], AppAgent [10], Mobile-Agent [11]).* These crop to one detected box or invoke an external "zoom" tool. They are not a budget-preserving partition of the whole screen, use no monotone partially-pooled map, have no collapse identity, and typically spend *extra* forward passes/compute (violating C1). Misses C1, C3, and C4.

- *(NM5) GUI-grounding pretraining and high-res branches (SeeClick/ScreenSpot [12], CogAgent [7]).* These improve grounding via data and pretraining, or by adding a dedicated high-resolution cross-attention *branch* (extra parameters/compute, violating C1). They are orthogonal to partition allocation, with no density-conditional partition and no collapse. Misses C1, C2, and C4.

- *(NM6) Free/learned glimpse and RL-crop selectors (recurrent visual attention, learned crop policies).* These can be per-region but are *unpooled free* selectors: unstable, not monotone-in-density, with no exact-collapse guarantee and often extra passes. Misses C3 and C4 (and often C1).

**Barriers.** Two assumptions kept the gap open. First, anyres sub-images were conceived as a *mechanism* for fitting elongated screens into a square encoder, never as a *budget* to be *allocated* by where UI elements are; the partition was a constant, not a covariate-conditioned function. Second, the field evaluates with pooled accuracy, which hides the density-skewed tail, so no one stratified by density to reveal the limitation or to attribute a fix to the density signal. Compounding these, the *triple* requirement - lift on dense screens, exact collapse on uniform screens, and zero added compute - is rarely targeted together, so adaptive-resolution methods that grow the budget or lack a collapse identity were considered "good enough."

**How LOUPE differs.** LOUPE is, to our knowledge, the first to (i) condition the anyres partition on an observable per-region UI element-density field (C2), (ii) realize it as a structured monotone, hierarchically partially-pooled map shrunk to the uniform prior (C3), (iii) keep the visual-token budget exactly fixed (C1), and (iv) guarantee feature-for-feature collapse to Ferret-UI at the default with a CI-gated E_collapse <= 1e-6 (C4). No prior line covers more than two of C1-C4.

**[Question 5] - What are the key components of my approach and results?**

*Method.* Replace Ferret-UI's fixed partition with the density-conditional allocation A_theta(S), holding the visual-token budget B fixed.

1. *Observable covariate rho.* Before the encoder, estimate a per-location element-density field rho(x, y) from a *cheap* source: (a) gradient/edge energy band-passed to UI glyph/icon frequencies; (b) class-agnostic small-box density from a light element detector; (c) OCR-box density; or (d) a tiny saliency head distilled from element-box annotations [13, 27]. Aggregate rho over candidate regions. The estimator adds negligible cost and runs once per screen (preserving C1).

2. *Knob K1 - density-equalizing split geometry (primary, token-neutral).* Place the pane boundary at s = s_mid + g_theta(Delta), where Delta is the signed density imbalance between the two candidate halves and g_theta is monotone non-decreasing with g_theta(0) = 0, clamped to valid bounds. Moving the boundary toward the denser side gives the dense (now smaller-area) pane a larger share of the *same* fixed encoder resolution - i.e., higher effective magnification - at exactly zero added tokens. Generalize to an adaptive grid by placing m-1 boundaries at density quantiles (uniform density -> equal spacing -> the fixed grid).

3. *Knob K2 - per-pane budget reallocation (optional, sum-preserving).* Set per-pane token budget t_i = t_global + h_psi(rho_i - rho_bar) subject to sum_i t_i = B, realized by choosing per-pane encoder resolution or token-merge ratio so totals match; h_psi monotone, h_psi(0) = 0, clamped. Dense panes get more tokens, sparse panes fewer, total unchanged.

4. *Partial pooling (C3).* theta and psi are a single shared functional form across screens. Per-screen density estimates are shrunk toward the uniform prior by a confidence weight (low density-variance or low-confidence screens shrink to equal halves); a hierarchical model (partial pooling [32]) across density-skew bins shrinks data-poor bins toward the global default. This prevents unpooled per-screen overfitting and makes the collapse natural.

5. *Exact collapse (C4).* At theta = psi = 0 (or whenever rho is flat), s = s_mid and t_i = t_global, so panes are byte-identical to Ferret-UI's; z_LOUPE = z_FerretUI; E_collapse <= 1e-6 is asserted in CI. K1 is differentiable via a soft-boundary resampler relaxed during training and snapped at inference; the snap is constructed to reproduce equal halves exactly at theta = 0.

*Training.* Reuse Ferret-UI's data and losses unchanged; train only theta, psi (and the optional saliency head) by backpropagating the existing referring/grounding/generation losses through the soft-boundary resampler, plus a small density-equalization regularizer and the budget constraint. No new labels.

*Datasets and benchmarks.* (1) Ferret-UI's elementary and advanced task suite on iPhone and Android screens (Android sourced from RICO [13, 1]); (2) ScreenSpot from SeeClick for GUI grounding across mobile/desktop/web with native icon-vs-text splits [12]; (3) Widget Captioning [27] and Screen2Words [26] for referring/summarization; (4) Spotlight-style tasks [8]; (5) optional Android-in-the-Wild for downstream action grounding [25]. Across all, compute a per-screen density-skew score (Gini/coefficient-of-variation of rho from ground-truth boxes) and stratify into deciles; also tag a small-element subset (elements below a pixel threshold).

*Baselines.* B1 global-image-only (no anyres); **B2 Ferret-UI fixed anyres (the collapse target)**; B3 uniform higher budget (more panes / higher resolution, compute-*unmatched* - an upper reference, since LOUPE must approach it on dense screens at B2's cost); B4 a finer fixed grid (e.g., 1x3, still density-blind); B5 covariate-scrambled LOUPE (shuffled density field); B6 unpooled per-screen free split (no pooling, no monotone constraint); B7 a ported global adaptive-slicing method (LLaVA-UHD / XComposer-4KHD style [19, 20]) re-implemented on the Ferret-UI backbone (adapts the global budget but not per-region density, no collapse); B8 per-screen oracle split (boundary grid-searched on validation accuracy - an impractical upper bound); B9 LOUPE (ours).

*Metrics.* PRIMARY: accuracy on the top density-skew decile, restricted to small-element tasks (icon recognition, find-icon, OCR/find-text on small text) and grounding localization (IoU / center-distance) on small targets. SECONDARY: density-skew slope (accuracy gain vs skew decile, expected monotone increasing); small-element subset accuracy; **pooled accuracy no-regression within +/- 0.3 points**; **compute parity** (identical visual-token count and encoder FLOPs vs B2, asserted); **E_collapse <= 1e-6**; and density-estimator calibration (how well rho predicts where errors concentrate).

*Ablations.* A1 K1-only vs K2-only vs K1+K2; A2 density source (edge vs detector vs OCR vs GT-box upper bound); A3 partial pooling on/off and shrinkage strength; A4 monotone constraint on/off; A5 budget-preserving vs budget-growing K2; A6 pane count / adaptive-grid granularity; A7 collapse-gate verification (theta = psi = 0 byte-identity).

*Falsifiability triples.* T1 *uniform-density null*: on spatially-uniform screens, LOUPE must equal B2 (no gain, byte-identical) - confirms collapse and that gains require skew. T2 *monotone dose*: gain must increase monotonically with density-skew decile; a flat or non-monotone curve refutes the mechanism. T3 *covariate-scramble*: shuffling rho must erase the gains; if gains persist, they come from extra capacity/regularization, not the density signal.

*Expected outcomes and hypotheses (all [conjectured, no preliminary results]).* H1: +3 to +10 points on top-decile small-element referring/grounding at fixed compute. H2: pooled accuracy no-regression within +/- 0.3. H3: positive, monotone gain-vs-skew slope (T2). H4: +5% to +15% relative improvement in small-target grounding IoU. H5: K1 alone (zero added tokens) captures the majority of the gain. H6: gains vanish under T1 and T3 (mechanism specificity). E_collapse <= 1e-6 holds by construction.

*Responsible AI and Data-Governance Considerations.* (F1, privacy) UI screenshots can contain PII/PHI/credentials (banking, health, messaging). Density estimation is geometric (edge/box density), runs on-device pre-LLM, and consumes no element *content*; raw crops are not logged; data minimization applies. (F2, proxy risk) Element density can correlate with app category, language, or script (e.g., dense CJK text); report density-stratified accuracy *also* across languages/scripts and app categories to ensure no group's small-element accuracy is disparately harmed. (F3, accessibility) Improving small-element grounding directly benefits assistive/screen-reader and motor-accessibility use cases (a pro-equity outcome); the exact-collapse default guarantees no regression on large-element/low-density screens. (F4, auditability) Log the chosen split/budget and density field per screen; the theta = psi = 0 disable switch reverts to audited Ferret-UI behavior, making the method shadow-deployable and contestable. (F5, integrity) All numeric hypotheses are marked conjectured; the E_collapse unit test is released for reproducibility.

---

## Section 4. References and Evidence

[1] K. You, H. Zhang, E. Schoop, F. Weers, A. Swearngin, J. Nichols, Y. Yang, Z. Gan. "Ferret-UI: Grounded Mobile UI Understanding with Multimodal LLMs." arXiv:2404.05719, 2024. (Source Introduction; anyres front end; elementary/advanced UI tasks.)
